column2: "Oui Pub" : ce qui va peut-être bientôt changer sur et dans votre boîte aux lettres
column3:
Pour limiter les déchets et le gaspillage de papier, le gouvernement souhaite lutter contre la prolifération des prospectus. Un dispositif va être expérimenté à compter du 1er septembre.

Voici un changement majeur qui pourrait bientôt intervenir sur vos boîtes aux lettres : il ne sera plus ...
column15: The article is about a French government initiative called 'Oui Pub' to reduce paper waste from advertising flyers/prospectus. While it touches on environmental concerns (waste reduction, paper waste), it does not substantively discuss climate change, greenhouse gas emissions, or climate policy. It is not about health topics either.

column2: Detta är EU:s gröna taxonomi - pengar ska investeras hållbart
column3:
Klimatet just nu kommer skickas till din mejlbox VARJE ONSDAG.

● Syftet med taxonomin är att guida investerare att placera pengar i hållbara verksamheter i stället för i verksamheter som är skadliga för klimat och miljö. Verksamheterna klassificeras utifrån sex fastställda miljömål.

● Investeringar i kärnkraft och naturgas ska enligt EU-kommissionens förslag som presenterades 2 februari 2022 klassas som gröna under vissa förutsättningar.

● Inom EU har åsikterna gått isär om kärnkraft och naturgas bör betraktas som klimatvänliga. Flera länder har tidigare hotat med att ta till rättsliga åtgärder om dessa klassas som hållbara. Att innefatta kärnkraft och naturgas har även sågats av kommissionens egen referensgrupp.

● Den svenska regeringen anser att kärnkraftsrelaterade verksamheter ska inkluderas, men är kritisk till att naturgasrelaterade verksamheter anses hållbara.

● I taxonomin ingår också att stora företag och finansiella aktörer ska redovisa hur stor del av verksamheten som definieras som miljö- och klimatvänliga.

● Kommissionens förslag ska även godkännas av EU-parlamentet och ministerrådet, alltså de 27 medlemsländerna. För att stoppa förslaget krävs ett nej från minst 20 medlemsländer med minst 65 procent av EU:s befolkning eller enkel majoritet i EU-parlamentet.

Läs mer:

Djup spricka mellan länder om kärnkraft och gas
column15: The article is about the EU's green taxonomy, which is a climate policy tool designed to guide investments toward sustainable activities and away from those harmful to climate and environment. It discusses classification of nuclear energy and natural gas as green/sustainable, which is directly related to climate change policy and energy transition. There is no health content in the article.

column2: Đorđe Balašević ostao je neokrunjeni kralj do kraja, bez obzira obožavali ga ili ne
column3:
Je li zaista moguće da je tek s Balaševićevom smrću nestala Jugoslavija, kako su se nakon njegove smrti izrazili mnogi govoreći o posljednjem utjecajnom trubaduru poteklom iz bivše države? Ili Jugoslavija i dalje postoji, naravno, kao neka idilična zamisao ili osjećaj koji je nekima još uvijek prisutan kao podsjećanje na, po njima, "bolju prošlost", dok su bili mlađi, ljepši i s više kose na glavi?

I što bi na to mogli reći Goran Bregović ili Zdravko Čolić koji, kvragu, valjda isto mogu reći da su značili i napravili puno u Jugoslaviji i ostali maskote zajedništva i "bratstva i djetinjstva"? Ili to zapravo svima njima nije ni bitno, koliko je bitno komentatorima nakon Balaševićeve smrti?

Ili se zapravo radi o naivnom bulažnjenju i romantičarskom razmišljanju, jer radilo se, jednostavno rečeno, o tadašnjoj političkoj datosti, ni dobroj ni lošoj, nego jednostavno takvoj kakva je bila u povijesnim okolnostima kakve su vladale i u kojima smo se našli? Znači, državi u kojoj smo živjeli, a nakon nje ostala su osporavanja ili nostalgična prisjećanja, ovisno kako ju je tko zapamtio.

Što je najbolje, ili najgore, mnogi koji se još nisu ni rodili do devedesetih danas o Jugoslaviji misle više, pa i bolje, od mnogih koji su u njoj živjeli. Otprilike po onoj logici da je svakoj novoj generaciji glavna psihološka fiksacija nešto o čemu su čuli, ali nisu tamo bili, poput nekog Woodstocka o kojem svi govore, a ja sam nažalost imao samo četiri godine kad je održan.

Možda je to nostalgija otprilike na način kakav osjećaju neki u Velikoj Britaniji, recimo, kao kad gledaju seriju "The Crown", pa vide kako je kraljica nekad putovala po svijetu, od Indije na dalje i bliže, posjećivala britanske kolonije koje su potom postale samostalne, a neki se sa suzom u oku sjećaju "onih boljih vremena", dok se neki sjećaju lošijih.

No Đorđe Balašević ostao je neokrunjeni kralj do kraja, bez obzira obožavali ga ili ne, i redovito putovao u sve novonastale države u regiji, od Vardara do Triglava, održavao rasprodane koncerte i zapravo je u njegovu slučaju izgledalo kao da se ništa i nije promijenilo što se tiče recepcije publike. Odgovarajući na neka pitanja o njegovu značaju za televizijske emisije nakon Balaševićeve smrti, rekao sam i da su zapravo najbitnije bile njegove pjesme i njihova vrijednost i privlačnost masovnoj publici, a ne ono što je netko naknadno, smisleno ili besmisleno htio "upisati" u njih.

Misliti drukčije značilo bi negirati osnovni poziv Đorđa Balaševića, a to je bilo pisati dobre pjesme i pametne knjige, bez obzira na ideološke pridjeve iz prošlosti, iako ga je, kao i sve ljude, i ta prošlost određivala. O čemu se i sam pozabavio govoreći i pišući o toj tematici. Uostalom, Balašević je u Jugoslaviji radio samo 12 godina, a zatim 30 u drukčijem političkom okruženju. Dapače, sve one naivne, "indoktrinirane" pjesme s političkom porukom iz tog razdoblja, poput "Računajte na nas" ili "Tri put sam video Tita", ostale su najmanje trajne i ponajmanje važne.

Možda je jedna od njegovih najvažnijih ranih pjesama bila "Neki novi klinci", samo se čini da su ti novi klinci bili mentalno "stariji" i okoštaliji i od njega. Ili, kako je jednom rekao Gandhi: "Budi promjena koju želiš vidjeti u svijetu". Mnogi su neskloni promjenama pa tako i autore koji su nastali u određenom vremenu non-stop trpaju u to isto vrijeme, od Balaševića do Čolića, Bregovića, Stefanovskog, Rade Šerbedžije i nedavno preminule Mire Furlan i drugih.

U velikoj mjeri radi se o nepravdi prema njima samima, jer su u posljednjih tridesetak godina rada iznjedrili mnoge uspjehe koji su umjetnički važniji, po životnim okolnostima u kojima su radili i značajniji, a čak i globalno važniji od onoga što su radili na početku karijera. No ta patetična nostalgija nikako da ode i uvijek će značiti nešto, ništa ili puno mnogima, ovisno kako je promatraju i kako svoja privatna značenja upisuju u nečiji rad, pa i onaj Balaševića ili Woodstocka.
column15: This article is a cultural commentary about the death of Đorđe Balašević, a famous Yugoslav/Serbian singer-songwriter, and discusses nostalgia for Yugoslavia, his artistic legacy, and how people project meanings onto artists and past eras. It has no content related to climate change or health.

column2: 'Ireland's not perfect but here's why we should be thankful - and happy Christmas to you all'  - Irish Mirror
column3:
Happy Christmas to one and all. I hope you all have a wonderful time with your loved ones this festive season.

Ireland may not be perfect but we have many things to be thankful for. We are not at war, we are not starving, and our climate is very mild and one of the safest in the world.

We are not under threat from either extreme heat, extreme cold or monsoons. Yeah we get far too much rain for our liking, but we learn to live with it.

We all know we don't have enough houses and the new ones are not being built fast enough. The level of poverty here is nothing compared to when I was growing up.

I remember lads going to school in their bare feet and very often their tummies aching for a half decent meal.

If you had no work, things were really tough because the money paid out on social welfare in those days was peanuts and wouldn't put food on the table.

People in those days didn't emigrate to go travelling for a couple of years to find themselves. They left the country because of rare poverty and for the chance of a better life abroad.

They knew when they went to somewhere so far like Australia or America that it was highly likely that they would never return home and in many cases might not see their parents or siblings ever again.

It wasn't that you couldn't get there by air or boats on holidays to seek them in these far away places but people simply could not afford it. It was just too expensive to travel unlike nowadays.

I am 63 years of age and the Ireland of today is a completely different place than when I was a kid.

Christmas always brings me back to my childhood and growing up in a two up, two down terrace house in Mary Street, Drogheda. My parents Johnny and Carmel dated since they were 14 year old and married young.

By the time I was ten years old there were five of us living in the little house but although we didn't have a lot, they were the happiest of times and the happiest of days.

My mother always had a big fire going in the kitchen. There was always food on the table. I can remember Christmas Eve, and all of us being sent to bed around eight o'clock.

My would look out the front door and tell us she just saw Santa flying across the sky causing great joy and excitement.

We would be down the stairs at 6am with the presents laid out across the floor of the little house. I remember one year getting a red car and I spent the whole of January going up and down the hill outside our home.

Neither of my parents drank and we were lucky that they were always there for us. On Christmas morning my mother would get us all dressed up and we would walk down the town to mass before going to see our grandparents. We all loved going to see the crib with the baby Jesus and singing all the Christmas carols.

One Christmas Eve dad got his first car and we got stuck in the snow on Barrack Lane, another steep hill not far from our home. We were all screaming in the back as the vehicle skidded all over the road, and my mother got us out and marched us all home to safety as we threw snowballs at each other.

They were simple times but the most wonderful of days. We later moved to a bigger house and there were two more additions to our family.

We are very lucky my mother is still alive and as busy as ever at 86 years of age.

My father is gone five years and I would give anything to have him back even for five minutes. He was so loved and much missed.

Each and everyone of us have our personal Christmas memories. But there will always be a special place in my heart for the little house where I spent my childhood.

As I always told my mother and father we were the lucky ones, we had the most fantastic childhoods and all the money in the world can never buy you happiness or love.

The birth of Jesus Christ is always worth celebrating. Christmas isn't about presents but spending time with those you love. Be grateful and have a great day.
column15: This is a nostalgic Christmas opinion piece about growing up in Ireland. While it briefly mentions Ireland's mild climate and lack of extreme weather threats, this is not a substantive discussion of climate change - it's just a passing reference to Ireland's favorable weather conditions. There is no substantive health or climate change content.

column2: High fees and lockdown blues: why students are in revolt
column3:
In her first year at the University of Bristol, Saranya Thambirajah has had little contact time for her politics course -- but plenty of hands-on experience mobilising protests and organising direct action against the institution's pandemic response.

The 19-year-old Londoner is one of the organisers of the 1,800 students on rent strike at the university. Forced into a fortnight of isolation after catching the virus last term, she found she was not so alone when it came to the frustrations she felt towards the university. Now she is part of a growing movement of students across the UK seeking to ensure that they don't become an afterthought in a higher education system scrambling to cope with waves of lockdown and social distancing.

Thambirajah is now back home, unable to return to university and halfway through a second term of studying without any in-person contact with her tutors. "I barely even know anyone on my course -- they're all just people on a computer screen," she says.

Despite the impact of the pandemic on teaching, social life, and mental and physical health, students are having to fight for the most basic rent reductions to university-owned accommodation; the cheapest room offered by the university costs £4,300 a year in rent. And all the while they are paying more than £9,000 in tuition fees every year.

As the second term continues, with students isolated at home, the long-term problems facing higher education are becoming all too apparent. Weighed down by steep tuition fees, and at a time of acute economic hardship for young adults, students are increasingly questioning the running of universities that many see as having been all but turned into businesses.

"All these rent strikes started as a response to the mishandling of the pandemic," says Thambirajah. "But I think now it's just exposing a lot of issues and flaws with the university systems that were already there."

When I left university in 2017, my generation wasn't exactly awash with optimism for the future. Setting to one side issues such as Brexit, which most students were passionately against, or climate change, more practical problems presented themselves: entry-level jobs that required years of experience; the impossibility of saving for the future; a rental market that feels less like a stepping stone towards the property ladder than a punishment for being born several decades too late.

Then, as now, student debt was regarded as so astronomical, and the interest rates applied to it so arcane, that it was better to ignore it and wait for it to be written off. Four years later, logging on to my student loan balance after a one-year post-graduate degree, the figure has ballooned to nearly £60,000. Watching these sums spiral is bad enough when you've enjoyed your university years. It is all the harder to accept for a cohort confined to their rooms and taught over Zoom.

Little wonder that dissatisfaction with the higher education experience is not confined to Bristol; rent strikes have sprung up at more than 50 UK universities since the beginning of the academic year, with different campaigns working together to maximise their impact.

The immediate priority of the group in Bristol is to ensure that students are granted rebates on their housing costs so far, whether or not individuals returned in the new year. Organisers want all students to get a 30 per cent reduction in rent for the full academic year, to recognise the disruption to their education and accommodation.

The administration views the situation differently. Robert Kerse, the university's chief operating officer, says that students living in residences will have received about a 25 per cent rebate over the course of the year, and points to bursaries set up for those with no other accommodation available. While he appreciates this has been "a particularly difficult" time for students, he adds, "we believe the university has gone above and beyond to provide support during this stressful and challenging period".

After a disastrous start to the autumn term when universities and the government encouraged anyone who could safely head to their halls to do so -- causing a surge in cases and forcing many halls into quarantine -- students have so far this year been discouraged from returning to campus.

A phased return to face-to-face teaching is reportedly being planned for March, but official guidance still states that, with the exception of courses such as medicine and veterinary science, students "should remain where they are wherever possible".

"It definitely feels like students have been forgotten," says Louis Holmes, another first-year Bristol undergraduate participating in the strike.

It is not just in England that students are up in arms. More than 50 US colleges, including George Washington University and NYU, face lawsuits from students seeking compensation for amenities closed during the pandemic. Last month, Paris's Left Bank saw a series of demonstrations drawing attention to poverty and declining mental health at universities. In Scotland, meanwhile, where education policy is handled by the devolved government, students in Glasgow protested against their treatment by erecting a fence around their vice-principal's lodgings.

Back in December, Thambirajah and a few others in Bristol began making WhatsApp groups to co-ordinate students' efforts. After getting in touch with the original organisers of the Rent Strike Now network, which began as a series of demonstrations in response to housing conditions at University College London in 2015, they have now inherited their website and social media channels.

The network hosted its first national rally online in January, with former shadow chancellor John McDonnell joining students, other Labour MPs and Jo Grady, University and College Union general secretary, to promote the rent strikes and call on the government to support students.

The Department for Education has allocated £70m in student support funds since December, aimed at covering living costs for those hardest hit by the effects of the pandemic. Having been encouraged to return to campus in the autumn, then subsequently blamed for a surge in infections in university towns, students have yet to be persuaded by government interventions.

"One minute we're expected to help the recession by contributing to the economy by going out, the next minute we're being scapegoated for a rise in cases because we did exactly what they told us to do," says Mattie Shannon, a final year student in disaster management at the University of Manchester. In November, she joined a group of students in Manchester who moved into the empty Owens Park tower on the Fallowfield campus, occupying it as part of a campaign to secure rent reductions for the remainder of the academic year. Banners fluttered from the building's windows and the occasional flare signalled the new residents' refusal to leave.

The two-week occupation helped secure a 30 per cent rent reduction for September to January (worth about £12m to the university), and the Manchester rent strike is now pushing for a 100 per cent rebate for accommodation costs incurred in the months before students are able to return safely, plus further reductions for the rest of the semester because of the reduced facilities available.

Now in the final year of her course, Shannon recognises in the grievances of freshers issues that she also experienced when she first arrived at the university: rodents, burst pipes, damp. "So long as they still get their rent, they won't make these improvements, which is why I think the rent strike is so vital -- to show that we're not always going to pay our rent."

Withholding rent is a powerful statement for her generation. According to the Resolution Foundation, during the first lockdown 14 per cent of under-30s missed a rent or mortgage payment, compared with 1 per cent of those aged 70 and over.

"When the economy booms, market forces can drive up rents very rapidly," says Chloe Timperley, author of Generation Rent, an investigation of the inequalities of the UK's housing market, published last year. "But when the economy crashes, those same market forces are overridden by the right to collect rent, which prevents the rental market from quickly adjusting to the new fundamentals."

Although the resulting financial difficulties affect both landlord and tenant, it is clearly the latter that is hit hardest. For Timperley, young people, and especially the captive audience of students, have become an "asset class" -- possessions of landlords rather than customers on an equal footing. The result is a significant disparity in treatment based on wealth: bad practices fall on financially precarious demographics who, lacking the luxury of choice, are unable to challenge them.

"Striking," she says, "is a last resort against a system that effectively treats [students] like cash cows."

A decade ago, students took to the streets to protest against plans to triple tuition fees in the UK to as much as £9,000 a year. Tens of thousands turned out amid rising tensions that saw 153 arrested on a single day of demonstrations in November 2010. The following month, the House of Commons approved the proposals by just 21 votes.

Polling suggests that the public has not since come round to the higher fees. A 2017 survey found that only 18 per cent of Britons supported the increase. Among 18 to 34-year-olds, it was 14 per cent. That year, Nick Clegg -- the former Liberal Democrats leader and deputy prime minister, seen as complicit in a decision he had once vowed to oppose -- lost his seat in the student-heavy constituency of Sheffield Hallam.

For the students participating in the rent strikes, tuition fees and accommodation costs are a reminder that what they believe should be available as a basic right now comes with a price tag. Almost all the students I speak to see the strikes as the inevitable product of an increasingly marketised higher education system.

"[They] wouldn't have happened if universities weren't run like businesses," says Dot, a second-year linguistics student participating in the rent strike at the University of Cambridge. "If they weren't run for profit, then they wouldn't be at risk of folding if we weren't here. And if they weren't at risk of folding then they could have prioritised what would have been better for student and staff health."

"You pay £27,000 to say you've got a degree," adds Ben McGowan, a first-year student in Manchester. "It's sort of just become a way to buy your way into a career."

While the cost of university has never been higher -- on average, tuition fees in England are second only to those in the US -- the value of actually going seems to be at an all-time low. In September, Boris Johnson took aim at what he sees as a mismatch of supply and demand in young people's qualifications. "We seem on the one hand to have too few of the right skills for the jobs our economy creates," the prime minister said, "and on the other hand too many graduates with degrees which don't get them the jobs that they want."

In recent years, the belief in the benefits of a steady increase in the proportion of university-educated adults, championed in the Blairite refrain of "education, education, education", has come under criticism. Gavin Williamson, the current education secretary, dismissed that Labour government's target for 50 per cent of young people to attend university as an "absurd mantra". In a speech last summer, he said: "From now on, our mantra must be 'further education, further education, further education'."

Yet the shortages of construction workers, mechanics, engineers and IT experts identified by Johnson in September have been affected by the pandemic. The month that Johnson announced his plans, there were 332,000 fewer jobs across the country overall than there were a year before.

"The crisis is unprecedented in many ways, but the long-term trends leading up to the crisis have definitely made us less prepared," says Maja Gustafsson, a researcher at the Resolution Foundation and co-author of the think-tank's latest "intergenerational audit".

As Britain endures the biggest economic slump on record, unemployment is hitting 16 to 24-year-olds harder than any other age group, with a 13 per cent increase of young people out of work since the pandemic began. Poor prospects for young people in turn mean slower recovery for the country as a whole.

"The way to build back better is to ensure that we have a sustainable recovery," says Gustafsson. "Sustainable economic growth just necessitates having all ages on board."

For young people to be at odds with their government is hardly a new phenomenon, but the extent of my generation's alienation may be unprecedented. The loss of these vital years elicits a range of reactions among my friends: anxiety, apathy, anger. The prospect of having to shoulder the financial burden of the pandemic despite little say in how it has been managed is overwhelming.

Faith in democracy is waning faster among young people than any other generation, while in the UK general elections of 2017 and 2019, it was age that denoted the biggest political divide. In 2019, the chance of voting Conservative increased by about 9 per cent with every decade added to a voter's age; the chance of them voting Labour, conversely, decreased by 8 per cent. Not even in the landslide election of 1997 is Tony Blair estimated to have attracted more than half of the 18-34 vote, which Jeremy Corbyn did in 2019.

Only 20 MPs have so far signed a motion supporting the strikers and recognising the "appalling treatment" of students: little wonder that half as many 18 to 24-year-olds view the UK government response to Covid-19 positively compared with those over 50.

"The government has shown that young people aren't their priority, and I think that's something we always knew," says Shannon. "But it has been made very clear in the last few months."

Spurred by such profound political disenfranchisement and economic disparity, student leaders are hoping rent strikes are only the start of a change in the balance of power at universities. As they begin to secure changes, the appeal of direct action could prove enduring.

"It has probably been more educational than my politics degree," says Bristol's Holmes. "But I wish I didn't have to do it."
column15: The article is primarily about UK university students revolting against high fees, rent costs, and poor pandemic responses. While it mentions mental and physical health impacts on students during the pandemic, these are brief mentions rather than substantive health content. Climate change is mentioned only in passing as one of several issues facing the generation. The article is fundamentally about student activism, higher education economics, and housing costs.

column2: 'Our beautiful mission': VanMoof boss on changing cities with e-bikes
column3:
In the 13 years since Ties Carlier and his brother started their e-bike company, it has grown to over 1,000 employees but, he says, there is a lot more to do to improve urban environments

The rebuilt Battersea power station in south-west London is an impressive architectural feat: its landmark towers were carefully deconstructed and then rebuilt while the derelict structure beneath was filled with modern conveniences. It has also received its fair share of opprobrium, with critics objecting to the privatisation, monetisation and gentrification the project has entailed.

All of which makes it an apt place to meet Ties Carlier, at the new London store of VanMoof, the e-bike company he founded with his brother in 2009. He, too, is out to reinvent an old classic, building it from the ground up for the modern era, injecting convenience and polish where others prefer the more rustic charm of what came before.

Like normal bicycles, e-bikes still need to be pedalled, but use a battery-powered motor to help the rider achieve speeds up to a UK legal maximum of 15.5mph. Carlier hails from the Netherlands - as his first name, height (he's 6ft 6in) and the fact that he's a cycling entrepreneur would suggest - but it was the Big Apple rather than Amsterdam that sparked the creation of his business.

"When my brother and I went to New York, we rode bikes, of course, but we saw that no one else was doing that. And the city was so perfect for it! So we thought, OK ... this is a beautiful mission: getting more people to ride bikes will improve cities, but it's also something we can make a business of."

Carlier and his brother Taco set out to redesign the pushbike for city commuting. The lights were integrated into the frame, so they couldn't be forgotten or stolen, and a chain lock retracted inside the bike when not in use. Their new design proved popular, but there was a catch. "We were selling out in New York and Tokyo, but we were selling to people who already love their bike. We couldn't grow out of the niche."

Where Amsterdam is small and flat and has a mild climate, their target markets were large, often hilly cities, where very cold winters or scorching summers put all but the most dedicated off cycling. The answer was electricity.

"Those elements are overcome by electric power. And that's when we realised that this can really enable cycling for everyone in the world - no matter how big your city is, no matter how many hills there are, the bike becomes the perfect, best way of travelling."

In the six years since its Electrified S bike hit the market, VanMoof has shipped about 150,000 and now employs just under 1,000 people. The design has evolved, too: the latest, the S5, launched in April, costs £2,998, and there is still as three- to four-month wait for delivery.

The company's expansion has changed Carlier's life in a more fundamental way, too: he met his wife, Cheryl, on a flight to Taiwan, where the bikes are manufactured, and the couple now live in Taipei with their small children.

But even as he tackles the standard growing pains of a young company - taking $128m of venture capital in 2021 to invest in a production process that aims to one day be capable of churning out as many e-bikes in a month as the company has sold since its founding - Carlier is still focused on addressing the pain points that stop so many from cycling.

VanMoof customers can, for example, pay £550 for its Peace of Mind service: a three-year programme offering free maintenance at its outlets and, more controversially, theft protection. The company promises to recover any stolen bike within two weeks or offer a replacement.

A YouTube series following the work of its "bike hunters" - who occasionally even steal bikes back from the thieves - went viral, but Carlier insists the goal is to make the theft protection unncecessary. VanMoof e-bikes lock solid with the touch of a button, they can be followed with GPS tracking until the battery runs flat, and unless the bike's owner sends an electronic code to unlock it, it would take a skilled mechanic to even turn the bike into an expensive pedal bike.

"In the beginning, in cities where we are not well known, rates of theft are indeed a bit higher. But in Europe, most bike are stolen by bigger, organised gangs, and these people realise very quickly, 'Oh, that bike. Don't steal it because we can't sell it. We can't use it.'"

However, although theft isn't a huge problem for VanMoof bikes, fear of it remains an issue. "People solve theft themselves by buying a cheap bike, because 'then it won't get stolen'. But it does get stolen, once or twice, so you buy an even cheaper one. Your riding experience gets worse and worse, so you ride less and less. And on top of that, it's not good for the environment."

Not every problem standing in the way of e-bikes is solvable with canny design, but that doesn't mean Carlier won't try. The company recently announced the VanMoof V, a chunky e-bike that is mechanically capable of going as fast as 31mph. Speed restrictions mean selling such a bike unlocked would be illegal in the UK, and most of Europe, but Carlier wants to provoke a conversation about whether those limits are always justifiable.

"We do think e-bikes should be faster in certain situations, to allow people to cover bigger distances on the bike," he says. His example is someone who lives in a suburb, who may be able to cycle perfectly safely at 30mph on wide arterial roads, before being limited to lower speeds when the bike detects that it has crossed into the urban core.

"In a real big picture, by enabling people to travel further by bike, you have fewer cars, and more space for those bikes. If you have something that actually brings you outside really easily and with a lot of fun, we could transform the city as we know it."

Age 43

Family Lives in Taipei in Taiwan with wife, Cheryl, and two young daughters

Education Car mechanic training in the Netherlands.

Pay "It's never been that important for me and my brother. Our business is our biggest hobby, so we like to keep as much in the company as we can."

Last holiday "We've been making holidays out of VanMoof store visits. Last year we went to six brand stores in Germany in one week."

Best advice he's been given "Stay fearless."

Phrase he overuses "If it was easy, everyone would do it."

How he relaxes Being on or near water.

Biggest career mistake "Underestimating how difficult it is to disrupt an established industry. I thought we would build a better bike, and then dominate the industry. But we're still at the beginning."
column15: The article is a profile of VanMoof's co-founder and the company's e-bike business. While there are passing mentions of environmental benefits ('not good for the environment', fewer cars, transforming cities), the article is primarily about the business, product design, and urban mobility rather than substantively about climate change. There is no substantive health content and no connection between climate change and health.

column2: " Parrains du libre-échangisme d'après-guerre, les Etats-Unis donnent l'exemple du renouveau protectionniste "
column3:
" Avant ", on aurait dénoncé, mine grave et ton outré, une vague de régression protectionniste - du temps où la doxa libre-échangiste imposait une mondialisation tous azimuts. Aujourd'hui, on parle savamment d'un retour en force de la " politique industrielle ". C'est une façon pudique pour les Etats de protéger de la concurrence un certain nombre de secteurs clés de la vie économique.

Personne ne s'y trompe. Quarante ans de globalisation universelle prennent fin. D'énormes richesses ont été créées. L'écart entre pays du Nord et pays du Sud s'est amoindri. Mais, à l'intérieur de chacun d'eux, d'énormes inégalités sont apparues.

Ajoutées à une série de chocs extérieurs - Covid-19, guerre, confrontation sino-américaine, retour de l'inflation, énergie chère -, elles ont déclenché un lent mouvement de correction. " Le balancier de l'histoire s'éloigne de l'intégration économique globale ", écrit Rana Foroohar, l'une des vedettes de l'analyse macroéconomique au Financial Times.

Dans deux domaines au moins, les Etats ne se cachent plus : on protège, ici et là, au niveau national, nombre d'instruments de la transition énergétique ; et on protège également certains segments de la haute technologie. Parrains du libre-échangisme d'après-guerre, les Etats-Unis donnent l'exemple du renouveau protectionniste.

Voté à l'été 2022, le Chips and Science Act a un double objet. Soumettant toutes les entreprises du high-tech américain à une autorisation d'exportation, l'administration démocrate entend retarder l'accès de la Chine aux semi-conducteurs les plus sophistiqués. Dans le même temps, Joe Biden veut doter les Etats-Unis - jusque-là dépendants de l'étranger, de Taïwan notamment - d'une capacité autonome de production de chips d'avant-garde.

Subventions et autres incitations publiques sont au programme. Aux objections de l'Organisation mondiale du commerce (OMC), l'administration répond : il en va des " intérêts de sécurité " de l'Amérique, qui ne doit pas se laisser distancer par la Chine. Le high-tech est dual, à usage civil et militaire. Les Chinois ont tiré les premiers. Dès 2013, ils ont programmé des centaines de milliards de dollars d'aide publique pour déployer, d'ici à 2025, la chaîne complète de fabrication des semi-conducteurs de demain. A l'OMC, à Genève, les vestales du libre-échange regardent leurs chaussures.

La lutte contre le réchauffement climatique nourrit la vague protectionniste. Avec un budget de 369 milliards de dollars (348 milliards d'euros) d'aides publiques diverses, l'Inflation Reduction Act, de Joe Biden, vise à faciliter la transition énergétique. On subventionne directement, on distribue des primes, on exempte d'impôt les fabricants de batteries et de voitures électriques à une condition - qu'elles soient, les unes et les autres, assemblées aux Etats-Unis. Finies les chaînes de valeurs compliquées : Biden réindustrialise à la maison.
column15: The article is primarily about the shift from free trade to protectionism, particularly in the US. It discusses the Chips and Science Act and the Inflation Reduction Act. Climate change is mentioned as a driver of protectionist policies (the IRA's $369 billion for energy transition), but the article's main focus is on trade policy and industrial policy, not climate change itself. The mention of climate change is substantive enough as it discusses energy transition funding and its role in protectionist measures. There is no health content in the article.

column2: 吳舜文新聞獎揭曉 嚴陳莉蓮：支持媒體人堅守專業 - 財經
column3:
第36屆吳舜文新聞獎在吳舜文新聞獎助基金會董事長嚴陳莉蓮、新北市長侯友宜等人見證下，公布八個大獎得主。嚴陳莉蓮強調，吳舜文新聞獎會一直堅持吳總裁成立基金會時的理念，支持及鼓勵所有媒體人堅守專業、迎向挑戰，成為穩定社會的重要力量。

基於企業社會責任與使命感，裕隆集團創辦人吳舜文總裁自1986年創設吳舜文新聞獎助基金會，秉持著新聞專業與公正客觀的精神，吳舜文新聞獎舉辦迄今邁向第三十六屆，以實際行動支持新聞從業人員，獎勵優質新聞作品，彰顯其對社會之貢獻及影響力。

嚴陳莉蓮開場致詞即表達了對所有入圍者的肯定，並感謝所有入圍者在這紛亂、變化迅速的時代，與基金會一同捍衛，堅持「提倡客觀與公正的新聞報導」的理念。嚴陳莉蓮強調，新冠肺炎、地緣政治、氣候變遷、能源危機、通貨膨脹等因素交雜重塑了世界，也帶來了許多的挑戰。在這樣的環境下，人民「知」的權利更顯重要。

同時，嚴陳莉蓮董事長也特別點出，好的理念需要傳承，因此從今年開始，新聞獎特別增設「影音類新聞深度報導獎--學生組 」，不僅鼓勵年輕學子延續傳承，也希望為未來的新聞領域注入創新的力量。

新北市長侯友宜擔任頒獎嘉賓，表示這是他第四次以市長身分參與這場盛會，每年來都帶給他滿滿感動，他特別推崇基金會36年來對新聞媒體的支持，也敬佩所有入圍者充分展現「好好做事情」的基本信念，讓新聞報導能夠從即時性到深度、從國際到台灣、從國家體制到社會的每個角落，多元化的報導不僅滿足了所有民眾知的權利，也讓政府能夠了解急需解決的問題，透過新聞同業努力呈現的真實面相，產生對土地更強的聯結感，然後能夠凝聚共識，繼續攜手往前走，讓這塊土地更加美好。

本屆評審總召集人蘇蘅表示，吳舜文新聞獎儼然是台灣最高新聞榮譽的標竿，今年參賽作品共422件，文字類263件，影音類159件，評委會先選出36件入圍作品，入圍率僅有8.5％，最終選出8項大獎得主，得獎率僅有1.8％，足見競爭的激烈性。

今年參賽作品也反映了台灣民主自由的豐富面貌，從聲張社會正義、突顯社會弱勢，到不畏戰爭險惡的國際前線，都可見到台灣媒體同業身影，並帶來新的視野，在質量上均有所提升。

蘇蘅也期許所有媒體同業要更了解新聞工作的職責，繼續挖掘並守護社會真相，做出公正客觀報導，對社會帶來民主活力，為民眾帶來希望。

擔任吳舜文新聞獎頒獎人 侯友宜：實質為社會注入元素 88擊案牽扯議長選舉 藍：掃黑從台南開始 綠批：未審先判 SASB準則實務專班 開班 #吳舜文新聞獎 #嚴陳莉蓮 #新聞 #社會 #基金會
column15: This article is about the 36th Wu Shun-Wen Journalism Award ceremony. While the speech by Yan Chen Li-Lian briefly mentions climate change, COVID-19, geopolitical issues, energy crisis, and inflation as factors reshaping the world, these are passing references to set context rather than substantive discussion. The article is primarily about the journalism award, its history, winners, and the importance of quality journalism.

column2: Comienza el ascenso en las temperaturas que desembocará en un "importante episodio cálido"
column3:
La llegada del anticiclón a partir de este jueves garantizará un tiempo estable, con cielos despejados y temperaturas en progresivo y generalizado ascenso, a lo que se sumará la llegada de una masa de aire cálido procedente del norte de África. De momento, la Agencia Estatal de Meteorología (Aemet) sigue hablando de un "importante episodio cálido", sin descartar que, a partir del fin de semana, se alcancen los umbrales de intensidad, extensión y duración requeridos para que se convierta en "la primera ola de calor del verano de 2023'', que se prolongará hasta mediados de la semana que viene, explica Rubén del Campo, portavoz de Aemet. Durante varios días, se superarán los 35° en buena parte del centro y sur de la Península y en áreas del norte y los 40° a 42° en los valles del Tajo, Guadiana y Guadalquivir, epicentro del episodio. Las noches que también serán muy cálidas y no se bajará de los 20° o 22° en la costa mediterránea y en el interior del centro y sur. Las temperaturas diurnas "estarán entre 5° y 10° por encima de los valores normales" para estas alturas del año.

Este jueves aún quedarán "restos de inestabilidad" en el Cantábrico oriental y el norte de Navarra, donde se esperan tormentas localmente fuertes que pueden ir acompañadas de granizo, aunque "irán a menos a lo largo del día". De hecho, hay avisos amarillos, el nivel más bajo de una escala de tres, en Aragón, Cataluña, Navarra y País Vasco por el riesgo de que se acumulen de 15 a 20 litros por metro cuadrado en una hora.

En el resto del país, cielos poco nubosos y temperaturas nocturnas "un poco más frescas", pero con máximas que "subirán en buena parte del país". "Los termómetros marcarán ya 36° en las horas centrales del día en el Guadalquivir, interior de la Comunidad Valenciana, Murcia y Mallorca", detalla el meteorólogo.

El viernes "subirán tanto las temperaturas nocturnas como las diurnas, salvo en puntos del área mediterránea, donde bajarán". Así, Madrid rondará los 33° y en Toledo se alcanzarán los 35°, mientras que Badajoz, Córdoba y Sevilla se acercarán a los 40°. Ese día se activarán los primeros avisos por calor del episodio, que serán amarillos y afectarán a áreas de Andalucía y Extremadura. En Galicia y el litoral cantábrico, las máximas alcanzarán valores superiores a los normales hasta el sábado, cuando podrían superarse los 34°- 36° en zonas del valle del Miño.

El fin de semana habrá "un nuevo ascenso generalizado". El sábado y el domingo subirán las temperaturas, aunque el domingo "es probable que bajen algo en el Cantábrico". Ambos días se alcanzarán los 35° en la mayor parte del centro y sur de la Península, en amplias zonas del nordeste y de la meseta norte; y los 40° a 42° en el Tajo, Guadiana y Guadalquivir. Por ciudades, hará más de 38° en Valladolid, Zaragoza, Zamora, Lleida y Madrid y 40° en Cáceres, Ciudad Real, Toledo, Jaén y Huelva, una cota que "con bastante probabilidad" se sobrepasará en Sevilla, Córdoba y Badajoz, que podrían rondar los 42° e incluso más. El sábado, último día al que llega el mapa de avisos, la alerta se mantiene en Andalucía y Extremadura y ya es naranja, el segundo escalón, en la campiña cordobesa y sevillana.

Y, cuando caiga el sol, haciendo mucho calor, con noches tropicales -- en las que las mínimas no bajan de 20° -- en amplias zonas del centro y sur de la Península, así como a orillas del Mediterráneo, donde se es descartable que en algún punto no se baja incluso de 25°, lo que se conoce como noches tórridas. Esta situación de altas temperaturas continuará "sin grandes variaciones, al menos hasta mediados de la próxima semana", aunque no se descarta algún chubasco aislado en zonas de montaña por las tardes. A partir ahí, los pronósticos apuntan, con la incertidumbre asociada al mayor plazo de predicción, a un descenso generalizado de las temperaturas, que volverían a valores propios de finales de junio.

En Canarias, los próximos días "soplarán los vientos alisios, con intervalos nubosos en el norte de las islas de mayor relieve y sin descartar alguna llovizna". A partir del lunes, el archipiélago se sumará a la fiesta nacional del calor con "una importante subida de las temperaturas" y valores que podrían alcanzar o superar los 35°.
column15: This article is about an upcoming heat wave in Spain in summer 2023, describing temperature forecasts and weather patterns in detail. It discusses high temperatures, anticyclones, and hot air masses from North Africa. While it mentions extreme heat, it does not connect this to climate change or global warming — it's a standard weather forecast article. There is no discussion of health impacts either, beyond describing uncomfortable conditions (tropical nights). No climate change framing or health content is present.

column2: Climate change a bigger crisis than Covid-19 pandemic, says SA expert
column3:
CAPE TOWN: The Covid-19 pandemic has revealed that governments around the world are able to deal with a global crisis, but there needs to be a sharper focus on the climate change, which is likely to have a greater impact.

This according to director of the development and rule of law programme at Stellenbosch University, Professor Oliver Ruppel, who says that while the pandemic has been destructive, green recovery can be extremely constructive and beneficial.

"Climate change is also a crisis, it's a catastrophe. It's something that is most likely going to be bigger than Covid-19 will ever be because it threatens our survival and threatens human nature and the future of our children," he said.

Ahead of COP26, the 26th United Nations Climate Change conference which is set to be held in Glasgow in November, Ruppel said the conference could hold accountable the countries that contribute the most to greenhouse gas emission.

"COP26 this year is going to, hopefully, be a special one in order for us to reach this 1.5 centigrade gap for us to stay alive."

MORE ON THISConcern with SA's reluctance to join plastic treaty after new reportProtecting the environment is not rocket science says Kenyan marathon legend KipchogeGovernment and energy groups support initiatives that will help alleviate SA's energy crisis

Ruppel served as a coordinating leader on the the Fifth Assessment Report of the UN's Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) in 2014.

"Recently, the sixth assessment report came and the findings were clearer than ever. It indicates that climate change is already taking its toll. We knew that, but it's becoming clearer and clearer in terms of the scientific data that we have. The effects of climate change are becoming irreversible," he said.

COP26 is set to put countries under obligation to reduce greenhouse gases in the atmosphere by means of their signature and ratification of the Paris agreement, which is an international treaty on climate change, adopted in 2015.

Ruppel said that while important issues like climate change and environmental protection are handled at a global level, local efforts play an integral role as well.

"At the local level is actually where the music plays, and that means citizens need to be educated and informed about the value of nature; about the need to protect it; about the opportunities that come with it when it flourishes and about the detrimental impacts if it doesn't."
column15: The article is primarily about climate change, comparing it to the Covid-19 pandemic in terms of crisis severity. It discusses COP26, the Paris Agreement, IPCC reports, greenhouse gas emissions, and the 1.5°C target. While Covid-19 is mentioned, it serves as a comparison point rather than being the substantive health topic. The article mentions climate change 'threatens our survival' but does not discuss specific health effects of climate change (like heat illness, disease expansion, etc.). The health connection is too vague to warrant the health or health_effects_of_climate_change labels.

column2: It took Zappone mess to clarify Covid rules are as clear as mud
column3:
There are a number of controversies. The confused and cosy appointment "process" might have been central in angering people, not her clear unsuitability for the office, despite Simon Coveney's cranky and unconvincing defence of her as a potential envoy of free expression. But it is her party for friends, civil servants and politicians -- an odd combination -- that reignited the affair.

Her private function in the Merrion Hotel appears to be a carbon copy of the Golfgate incident that saw Dara Calleary resign from Cabinet last summer. Both might have been within the regulations, but not the spirit of the regulations.

There was again a sense of one rule for them and another for us. The hypocrisy was especially galling as Tánaiste Leo Varadkar had been toying with people with his continuous speculation on the size of weddings, and then there was another minister's exaggerated criticism of Catholic bishops for proceeding with communions as "putting lives at risk".

Lots of people took to social media to point out how they had suffered earlier in the year, postponing weddings, having funerals limited to inhumane numbers that saw close family members excluded.

But their sacrifices, while very real, are not comparable. That was when we either did not know much about the virus or it was running out of control. Comparing what was allowed then to what is allowed now is meaningless. Things have changed.

The real disgrace of the Zappone affair is not the hypocrisy of the Government or the unfairness of the rules. The disgrace is that it took the Attorney General Paul Gallagher to clarify what the law is on gatherings.

A key principle of the rule of law is that "the laws are clear, publicised, stable and fair". There is a lot of publicity on what we can and cannot do, and though it changes all the time there are good reasons for that. Fairness is another issue. But that it took the AG's intervention to establish what the law says suggests it is anything but clear.

Before that Varadkar proclaimed hopefully that the event he attended was allowed under Government regulations. Others pointed to Government advice and Bord Fáilte guidance to say he was wrong. The confusion emanates from the difference between the guidance and the law.

For most of the early part of the pandemic the Government relied on people adhering to advice -- staying at home, closing businesses, limiting travel. In the autumn there was a move to put a legal basis to many of the restrictions, but these often differed from the Government guidance. Guidance was and still is stricter than the law.

So there appears never to have been a legal ban on celebrating mass, though most of us would be forgiven for thinking there was. The Government might have reasonably thought no such ban would have survived a court challenge given the clearly defined right to practice religion in the Irish Constitution.

The regulations then, Mr Gallagher was able to point out, were relatively clear. But they were not clear enough to feed through into guidance. The Restaurants Association of Ireland said it was unaware groups of up to 200 could be accommodated outdoors. It was only after the Zappone function that Bord Fáilte updated the guidance to allow for larger outdoor events.

One Government TD, the Green Party's Neasa Hourigan, has even said the AG's clarification is "incredibly unhelpful" because, while "technically correct" it sends the message we're all allowed to organise a party in a field. This patronising attitude suggests she feels the lower orders can't be trusted, and even if we can't ban things we'll tell them they are banned.

A good outcome from this mess might be that Government advice will now reflect the legal reality, giving people, clubs and business owners more certainty. The other thing we can see is most people and businesses are already more relaxed. The fact the Merrion Hotel allowed a relaxed, informal gathering at which people were free to mix and move around shows the political class is doing what the rest of us already are.

Most people out and about in Ireland are behaving normally with each other. House parties are happening again. The Government needs to update regulations to reflect that, and to enable other activities. Many office buildings remain closed. Libraries don't allow us browse shelves. Live music and theatre is still severely restricted.

This is despite the Government celebrating the vaccine roll-out. The real success story of the roll-out is that almost everyone in Ireland is willing to take a vaccine. Why we have lower vaccine hesitancy than other places is open to speculation, but it's allowed us overtake other countries which had a head start, and there is a good chance Ireland will have the highest vaccine coverage in the world by the end of this month.

But what's the point of having a world-beating vaccine programme if we can't organise a Park Run, not to mind the Dublin City Marathon in October? There are no sessions in pubs here yet other European countries are back to holding music festivals. Stadia are practically empty. The UK, which was accused of taking a reckless approach when it removed almost all restrictions, has not seen unbearable pressure on its hospitals.

We don't have to declare a 'Freedom Day', but it is time that Ireland relaxed its rules, and ensured the guidance reflects the rules. However, as the Government lurches from mini-crisis to mini-crisis there is still no sense of a strategy, just an abundance of caution and a paucity of sense.
column15: This article is primarily about the political controversy surrounding Katherine Zappone's appointment and party in Ireland, and the confusion around COVID-19 regulations versus guidance. It discusses pandemic restrictions, vaccine rollout, and public health measures, which falls under the health category. There is no discussion of climate change or its health effects.

column2: Capoulas Santos relativiza problema da seca e lembra investimentos no Alqueva
column3:
O antigo ministro da Agricultura Luís Capoulas Santos apelou a que se relativize o problema da seca, lembrando o registo histórico de longos e frequentes períodos sem chuva, particularmente no sul do país, e mostrando-se desfavorável a uma revisão dos acordos hídricos com Espanha, visto que estes favorecem, na sua opinião, o nosso país.

Em entrevista à Antena 1 e ao ‘Jornal de Negócios’, o deputado do PS ressalvou que a questão da seca “não é nova”, recordando que as observações meteorológicas mostram que costuma haver períodos de alguns anos de seca “a cada dez, treze anos”.

“Nesta matéria, muito tem sido feito para combater a seca. Fez-se o maior investimento público em Portugal, o Alqueva, que tem neste momento um investimento de fundos públicos de mais de dois mil milhões de euros e o privado mais de quatro mil milhões de euros […]. Acho que temos de ser cautelosos”, afirmou Capoulas Santos.

Para o antigo governante, não faz sentido Portugal equacionar reabrir negociações com Espanha sobre a gestão dos recursos hídricos; ao invés, o acordo em vigor “é-nos altamente favorável” e qualquer revisão suscita em Capoulas Santos “dúvidas que consigamos obter resultados melhores do que aqueles que já dispomos.”

“O que se trata é de uma franca cooperação e uma monitorização, porque não basta que os nossos vizinhos espanhóis cumpram as quantidades a que estão obrigados, o que interessa é que as cumpram de forma regular e distribuída ao longo do ano”, alertou.

O atual deputado socialista aproveitou ainda para sublinhar a necessidade de respeitar “a confiança dos cidadãos e dos empresários” que resultou na maioria absoluta do seu partido, falando num grande capital “que não pode ser desbaratado”. Para o antigo ministro da Agricultura, a responsabilidade que recai agora nos ombros do Executivo e, sobretudo, de António Costa não pode ser desperdiçada, mas afastou-se de qualquer compromisso com a pasta que já ocupou.

Ainda assim, um Governo “mais pequeno, mais ágil” poderia ser benéfico e algo com que concordaria, admitindo mesmo “várias configurações possíveis” para uma fusão da pasta da Agricultura com outros ministérios.
column15: The article discusses drought in Portugal and water management investments (Alqueva), as well as water agreements with Spain. While drought can be related to climate change, the article frames it as a historically recurring phenomenon and focuses on political and infrastructure responses rather than connecting it to climate change. There is no mention of climate change, greenhouse gases, or global warming. There is also no health content.

column2: يؤدي إلى الوفاة عند كبار السن.. علامات الالتهاب الرئوي | المصري اليوم
column3:
الالتهاب الرئوي هو عدوى تصيب أنسجة الرئة وتتسبب في التهاب الحويصلات الهوائية وامتلائها بالسوائل أو القيح. والالتهاب الرئوي هو عدوى تسببها فيروسات أو بكتيريا أو فطريات تؤثر على جزء أو أكثر من الرئتين.

الالتهاب الرئوي يمكن له أن يصيب الأطفال، وكبار السن، والأشخاص الذين يعانون من ضعف المناعة بشكل خاص، وقد يكون الالتهاب الرئوي خفيفًا أو خطيرًا حسب العمر والحالة الصحية العامة، لذا وفقًا لموقع "Everyday Health" التعرف المبكر على الأعراض يُعد أمرًا بالغ الأهمية لتلقي العلاج المناسب وتجنب المضاعفات.

- السعال المصحوب ببلغم، غالبًا ما يكون البلغم كثيفًا، ويميل لونه إلى الأخضر أو الأصفر، وفي بعض الأحيان قد يكون ممزوجًا بالدم.

- الحمّى والقشعريرة، ارتفاع درجة الحرارة مع تعرّق غزير، وأحيانًا رجفة شديدة.

- ضيق التنفس، يشعر المريض بصعوبة في التنفس، خاصة عند القيام بأي مجهود بسيط.

- ألم في الصدر، يظهر الألم أثناء التنفس أو السعال، وغالبًا ما يكون حادًا ويشبه الطعنات.

- الإرهاق العام، يشعر المصاب بضعف عام في الجسم، وصعوبة في أداء الأنشطة اليومية.

- تسارع ضربات القلب والتنفس، نتيجة انخفاض الأكسجين في الدم.

- زرقة الشفاه أو الأظافر، تدل على نقص الأوكسجين في الجسم.

- اضطرابات عقلية عند كبار السن مثل الارتباك أو انخفاض مستوى الوعي، وهي علامة خطيرة تتطلب تدخلاً عاجلاً.

column2: Опасен ли е парацетамолът? Защо Тръмп го свърза с аутизъм и какво казват лекарите
column3: Темата накратко EMA и FDA подчертават, че парацетамолът остава важна и най-безопасна опция за лечение на болка и температура при бременни жени, като нелекуваната висока температура може да е по-опасна за майката и плода. Увеличението на случаите на диагностициран аутизъм през годините се обяснява с разширяването на дефиницията на "разстройство от аутистичния спектър" и по-голямата информираност, а не с конкретни външни фактори като парацетамол. Най-скорошното проучване от 2024 г. в Швеция не открива връзка между парацетамола и аутизма, когато се изследват братя и сестри, като подчертава влиянието на гените и факторите на околната среда. Парацетамолът е широко използвано нестероидно противовъзпалително средство, което се съдържа в множество лекарства без рецепта в България, но употребата му трябва да е с повишено внимание поради възможни странични ефекти при предозиране. Лекарите съветват бременните жени да използват парацетамол само при необходимост, в най-ниската ефективна доза и за възможно най-кратък период от време, и винаги след консултация с лекар. Тази седмица американският президент Доналд Тръмп успя да втрещи международни здравни организации, лекари и учени, след като съобщи на брифинг в Белия дом, че парацетамолът и ваксините предизвикват аутизъм. Той не цитира никакви научни изследвания или здравни експерти, за да подкрепи твърдението си. Архивът на "Капитал" Статията, която искате да прочетете, е част от архива на "Капитал", който е достъпен само за абонати . Той включва над 200 000 статии с всичко за бизнеса, политиката и обществото в България от 1993 г. насам. Вече съм абонат Абонирайте се и получете пълен достъп до богатото съдържание на Капитал. 1.96 лв/ 1.00 € на седмица Към офертата Вижте другите ни предложения * Абонаментът Ви ще бъде подновяван автоматично и таксуван на месечна база. Може да прекратите по всяко време. Цена за първите 3 месеца 7.84 лв. / 4 € , след това 29.34 лв / 15 € месец.
